epics.title: Phase 1: CLI polish — six iteration-pass additions
Phase 2: forge tasks edit — editor-mode for humans

tasks.title: Land named constants + Phase 1 dispatcher tokens
tasks.description: Prerequisite for all other Phase 1 tasks. Adds three named constants and grows the subcommand dispatcher's RESERVED / VALUE_FLAGS / BOOLEAN_FLAGS arrays so each feature task (delete, --blocked-by, ready filter, show children, epic --id) has a stable base to wire handlers into. Handlers themselves are NOT wired by this task — that is each feature task's job. This split keeps Phase 1 feature tasks parallelizable once this lands.
acceptance: GATE_LABEL_HUMAN, PHASE_LABEL_PREFIX, COMMIT_PLAN_TEMPLATE exported from src/lib/tasks/types.ts as string constants
src/commands/tasks.ts RESERVED array contains 'delete'
src/commands/tasks.ts VALUE_FLAGS set contains '--blocked-by', '--phase', '--id'
src/commands/tasks.ts BOOLEAN_FLAGS set contains '--confirm', '--children', '--full'
Dispatcher does NOT yet contain 'edit', 'gate', '--editor', or '--dry-run' (those are Phase 2 / Phase 5)
pnpm typecheck passes
pnpm test passes (no new tests required for this scaffolding task; existing tests must still pass)
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/types.ts (modify), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify). No test additions expected; existing tests must stay green.

tasks.title: Implement forge tasks delete with descendant-scan
tasks.description: Add forge tasks delete <task-id> --confirm subcommand. Without --confirm, prints a preview and exits non-zero. With --confirm, removes the task atomically AND cleans up any dangling dependencies (other tasks that list the deleted task in their dependencies[] get it removed). Refuses if the task has any descendants (matched by ID prefix, same pattern as container detection in getReadyTasks).
acceptance: forge tasks delete FORGE-X.Y without --confirm prints a preview (task title + id + descendants count) and exits non-zero
forge tasks delete FORGE-X.Y --confirm on a task with no descendants removes it from tasks.json atomically
After deletion, any other task that previously listed FORGE-X.Y in its dependencies[] no longer does
forge tasks delete FORGE-X --confirm on a task with descendants (e.g. FORGE-X.1 exists) exits non-zero with a message naming the descendants; tasks.json is unmodified
forge tasks delete UNKNOWN-ID --confirm exits non-zero with a clear error; tasks.json is unmodified
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover: happy delete + dep cleanup, descendant refusal, unknown id, preview without --confirm
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover the subcommand wiring (stdout preview, exit codes)
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts (modify — add deleteTask), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — add handler), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). Descendant pattern: see getReadyTasks container detection at src/lib/tasks/queries.ts:113-128.

tasks.title: Extend forge tasks create with --blocked-by flag
tasks.description: Add repeatable --blocked-by <id> flag to forge tasks create. Each value is pushed onto the new task's dependencies[] at creation time. Unknown blocker ID fails creation atomically — no partial writes to tasks.json. Existing invocations without --blocked-by must continue to work unchanged.
tasks.design:
In src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts, extend createTask to accept an optional blockedBy?: string[] field on its options object.

Blocker-id validation: before writing, build a Set<string> of all existing task ids by running discoverTaskFiles (queries.ts) + readTasksFile over every file, collecting every task.id across all features (same discovery pattern getReadyTasks uses to build its statusMap — see queries.ts:96-111). For each id in blockedBy, require id ∈ allIds. If any id is missing, throw a single error that names every unknown id (batch the failures so the user sees them all at once). The exception path must leave tasks.json untouched on disk.

When blockedBy is present and all ids resolve, set the new task's dependencies = [...blockedBy] before persisting. When blockedBy is absent or empty, dependencies = [] (unchanged from today).

In src/commands/tasks.ts: parse --blocked-by as a repeatable VALUE_FLAG (already in VALUE_FLAGS from FORGE-3.1). Collect all occurrences into a string[] and pass to createTask({ blockedBy }). Follow the exact repeatable-flag collection pattern already used for --acceptance (-a) and --label (-l) in the existing handler.
acceptance: forge tasks create <feature> 'title' --blocked-by FORGE-X.Y --blocked-by FORGE-X.Z creates the task with both IDs in its dependencies[] array
forge tasks create <feature> 'title' --blocked-by UNKNOWN-ID exits non-zero with a message naming the unknown id; tasks.json is unmodified
Existing forge tasks create invocations without --blocked-by produce byte-identical tasks.json output (no dependencies[] change) as before
Single --blocked-by works (degenerate case of repeatable)
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover: multi-value happy path, single-value happy path, unknown id rejection, cross-feature blocker resolution
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover the flag parsing and end-to-end invocation
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts (modify — extend createTask signature and implementation), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — collect repeated --blocked-by values and wire to createTask), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). The --blocked-by VALUE_FLAG entry is already present from FORGE-3.1.

tasks.title: Extend forge tasks ready with --label and --phase filters
tasks.description: Extend forge tasks ready with a repeatable --label <L> flag (AND semantics when passed multiple times) and a --phase <N> sugar flag that translates internally to --label ${PHASE_LABEL_PREFIX}${N}. Filters compose (--phase 5 --label needs-design returns tasks with BOTH labels). Existing forge tasks ready with no filter flags returns all ready tasks unchanged (backward compat).
tasks.design:
In src/lib/tasks/queries.ts, extend getReadyTasks signature to accept an optional filter: { labels?: string[] }. After the existing leaf/open/deps-satisfied filter (queries.ts:93-165), apply a label filter: for each label L in opts.labels, require L ∈ task.labels. Multiple labels AND together (all must be present).

In src/commands/tasks.ts ready handler:
  - Collect all --label occurrences into a string[] (VALUE_FLAG already present per FORGE-3.1).
  - If --phase N is also passed, append `${PHASE_LABEL_PREFIX}${N}` to the same list. Use the PHASE_LABEL_PREFIX constant from src/lib/tasks/types.ts (landed by FORGE-3.1) — do NOT hardcode 'phase:'.
  - Pass { labels } to getReadyTasks.

No schema change. Phase remains a label convention.
acceptance: forge tasks ready --label gate:human returns only tasks whose labels[] contains 'gate:human'
Two --label flags intersect: forge tasks ready --label frontend --label needs-design returns tasks with BOTH labels
forge tasks ready --phase 5 returns the exact same result as forge tasks ready --label phase:5
forge tasks ready --phase 5 --label needs-design returns tasks with both phase:5 and needs-design labels
forge tasks ready (no filter flags) returns all ready tasks — identical output to pre-task behavior
forge tasks ready --label no-matches returns an empty list (and empty JSON array with --json)
Phase handler references PHASE_LABEL_PREFIX from types.ts (no hardcoded 'phase:' string in tasks.ts)
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover: getReadyTasks with single label, multiple labels (AND), no matches, backward compat with no filter
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover --label / --phase / combined flag handling
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/queries.ts (modify — extend getReadyTasks), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — ready handler), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). --label and --phase VALUE_FLAG entries are already present (--label pre-existing; --phase from FORGE-3.1).

tasks.title: Extend forge tasks show with --children and --full recursion
tasks.description: Extend forge tasks show <task-id> with two new booleans: --children (renders direct children in a nested summary block) and --full (when combined with --children, recurses through all descendants and renders every field — description, design, acceptance, notes — instead of the default summary). --full alone (without --children) preserves existing behavior for a single task. JSON output (--json --children --full) emits structured nested children arrays.
tasks.design:
Add helper in src/lib/tasks/queries.ts:

  export function getDescendants(
    parentId: string,
    scope: 'direct' | 'all',
    cwd?: string,
  ): Task[];

Direction: from parent DOWN (distinct from the existing container-detection at queries.ts:113-128, which walks leaves UP to mark ancestors). Implementation is the simple inverse: iterate all tasks and pick those whose id starts with `${parentId}.`.

  - scope 'direct': filter further to children exactly one dot-level deeper. Example: parentId 'FORGE-3' → matches 'FORGE-3.1', 'FORGE-3.2' but NOT 'FORGE-3.1.1'. Check by counting dots in the suffix after `${parentId}.`.
  - scope 'all': every descendant at any depth. No dot-count filter.

Returns Task[] sorted by id ascending (stable output for tests).

Extend the show handler (handleShow) in src/commands/tasks.ts:
  - Default (no --children): existing behavior unchanged.
  - --children (summary): call getDescendants(id, 'direct'). Render parent, then an indented summary block for each child (id + title + status). If the result is empty, emit a single literal line '  (no children)'.
  - --children --full: call getDescendants(id, 'all'). Render the parent and every descendant, each with full fields (description, design, acceptance, notes). Preserve existing --full field rendering for the parent. Sort by id so nested ordering is deterministic.
  - --json variants: emit structured { task, children: [...] } with each child optionally having its own children array. For --children --full, nest recursively.

--children and --full are BOOLEAN_FLAGS already landed by FORGE-3.1.
acceptance: forge tasks show FORGE-X --children renders the parent task followed by its direct children in an indented summary block (id + title + status per child)
forge tasks show FORGE-X --children --full renders the parent and all descendants recursively, each with full fields (description, design, acceptance, notes)
forge tasks show FORGE-X --full (without --children) behaves identically to existing --full (no regression on the parent-only view)
forge tasks show FORGE-X --children on a task with no children renders the parent followed by an empty-children indicator (e.g. '  (no children)')
forge tasks show FORGE-X --json --children --full emits nested JSON: { task, children: [{ task, children: [...] }] }
forge tasks show UNKNOWN-ID --children exits non-zero with an unknown-id message
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover getDescendants with direct vs all scopes, including empty-children case
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover --children, --children --full, JSON output shape, and empty-children rendering
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/queries.ts (modify — add getDescendants), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — show handler), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). --children / --full BOOLEAN_FLAG entries are already present from FORGE-3.1.

tasks.title: Extend forge tasks epic create with --id flag
tasks.description: Extend forge tasks epic create to accept --id <explicit-id>. With --id, the epic is created with the specified ID; the duplicate-ID check runs inside the existing .epic-lock critical section (NOT by lock contention — by an existence scan performed while the lock is held). Without --id, current auto-increment behavior is unchanged. Malformed IDs (wrong prefix, non-numeric suffix) rejected before any lock is acquired.
acceptance: forge tasks epic create <feature> --id FORGE-9 'title' creates an epic with id exactly 'FORGE-9'

A second forge tasks epic create anywhere in the project using --id FORGE-9 is rejected by the duplicate-ID existence scan inside .epic-lock; error message names the conflicting epic (feature + title); tasks.json is unmodified
forge tasks epic create <feature> --id BADFORMAT 'title' rejected with a shape-validation error BEFORE the lock is acquired; tasks.json is unmodified
forge tasks epic create <feature> 'title' (no --id) uses existing auto-increment behavior unchanged
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover: happy explicit-id path, duplicate-detection-under-lock path, malformed-id rejection, backward-compat auto-increment path
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover the --id flag wiring and error messages
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts (modify — extend createEpic), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — epic create handler), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). --id VALUE_FLAG already present from FORGE-3.1. Existing .epic-lock logic lives at src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts:36-86.

tasks.title: Add --replace flag to forge tasks update --acceptance
tasks.description: Add a --replace boolean flag to forge tasks update. When present with --acceptance (-a), the provided acceptance values replace the task's acceptance[] array instead of appending to it. Default behavior (without --replace) stays append — backward compatible with every existing invocation. This closes reviewer issue #3 (append-only --acceptance) independent of Phase 2's editor-mode, providing a cheap fallback if Phase 2 slips.
tasks.design:
In src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts, extend the existing updateTask options to accept a replaceAcceptance?: boolean field. When replaceAcceptance === true AND the incoming acceptance value is present, overwrite task.acceptance with the new array. When false/absent, preserve current append behavior.

In src/commands/tasks.ts:
  - Add --replace to BOOLEAN_FLAGS set (net-new Phase 1 token; FORGE-3.1 did NOT add this).
  - In the update handler: if --replace is set, pass replaceAcceptance: true to updateTask.
  - --replace paired with --acceptance triggers replacement. --replace without --acceptance is a no-op (explicit note in --help; exit 0, no write).
  - Do NOT generalize --replace to other repeatable fields (--label, etc.) in this task. Scoped to --acceptance only; further fields are out of scope for V1.

Note: this task is the ONE exception to the FORGE-3.1 rule that 'dispatcher tokens all land in 3.1' — --replace wasn't foreseen at 3.1's decomposition, and it's small enough to land in the same task as its handler wiring. Call this out in the PR description for clarity.
acceptance: forge tasks update FORGE-X.Y --acceptance 'first' --acceptance 'second' --replace sets task.acceptance to exactly ['first', 'second'], regardless of prior contents
forge tasks update FORGE-X.Y --acceptance 'added' (no --replace) preserves existing append behavior — every existing invocation produces byte-identical results to pre-task
forge tasks update FORGE-X.Y --replace (no --acceptance) is a no-op — exit 0, no write, no error
BOOLEAN_FLAGS in src/commands/tasks.ts contains --replace
Unit tests in src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover: replace happy path (pre-existing acceptance overwritten), append backward-compat (no --replace), --replace without --acceptance no-op
Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts cover the flag parsing and end-to-end invocation
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes
tasks.notes: Files: src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts (modify — extend updateTask options), src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — add --replace to BOOLEAN_FLAGS and wire in update handler), src/lib/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). This task closes reviewer issue #3 independent of Phase 2. If Phase 2 ships first, --replace still remains a useful scripting path for agents that prefer one-shot flag invocations over editor-mode.

tasks.title: Editor module — buffer render, parse, hash, and roundtrip fixture
tasks.description: Create src/lib/tasks/editor.ts with pure functions that transform Task ⇄ markdown-with-YAML-frontmatter. No I/O beyond the test fixture. This is the foundation every other Phase 2 task builds on.
acceptance: renderBuffer(task) produces a string containing the non-editable header, YAML frontmatter with '---' delimiters, and the four body sections in the exact order Description / Design / Acceptance / Notes
parseBuffer(renderBuffer(task)) returns a ParseResult whose task content is equal to the input's editable-field subset (title, priority, labels, dependencies, description, design, acceptance, notes)

Round-trip test: for every task in src/lib/__tests__/fixtures/tasks-roundtrip.json, parseBuffer(renderBuffer(task)) returns an equivalent task (by value) for all editable fields; hashTask(task) === hashTask(parseBuffer(renderBuffer(task)).task) for every fixture task
parseBuffer throws with a message naming the unknown key when frontmatter contains an unknown field
parseBuffer throws with a message naming the unknown section when the body contains a section other than Description/Design/Acceptance/Notes
parseBuffer captures '- [x]' lines as acceptance text and emits a warning in ParseResult.warnings; the warning string contains 'check state is not stored'
hashTask is deterministic: hashTask(t) === hashTask(t) across calls; two tasks differing only in title produce different hashes
src/lib/__tests__/fixtures/tasks-roundtrip.json contains tasks covering every shape combination listed in the design
src/lib/__tests__/tasks-editor.test.ts is created with the tests covering render, parse (including error cases), roundtrip, hashTask determinism, and [x] warning
src/lib/tasks/index.ts re-exports renderBuffer, parseBuffer, hashTask, and the ParseResult type
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes

Additionally: the canonical render path is byte-deterministic — render(parse(render(task))) produces output byte-identical to render(task). This is stronger than value-equivalence and catches whitespace/newline drift between parse and render.

Parser robustness — user markdown may contain '##' sub-headings inside body sections. Splitting rule: recognize ONLY the four literal section-start lines '## Description', '## Design', '## Acceptance', '## Notes'. Any other '## <anything>' line is content within the CURRENT section, NOT a new section. Unit-test this with a fixture task whose description contains '## Edge cases' and '## Example output' — parse must keep both inside description.

Parser rejection — non-editable fields in frontmatter. If the frontmatter contains any of the reserved key names 'id', 'status', 'created', 'closeReason', 'comments' (fields displayed in the reference header but NOT editable), throw a specific error: 'field <name> is non-editable; it appears in the reference header for display only. Remove it from frontmatter.' Do NOT fall into the generic 'unknown key' branch — users may reasonably assume frontmatter is the editable surface and need a tailored hint.
tasks.notes:
Files: src/lib/tasks/editor.ts (create), src/lib/tasks/index.ts (modify — re-export), src/lib/__tests__/fixtures/tasks-roundtrip.json (create), src/lib/__tests__/tasks-editor.test.ts (create — flat file, NOT a nested __tests__ dir).

YAML parsing: use 'gray-matter' (already in package.json deps). gray-matter parses frontmatter natively and handles YAML via its bundled parser — do NOT add js-yaml as a new dep. For the body (post-frontmatter markdown), split sections with a simple regex on /^## /m — no markdown parser needed for Phase 2.

Scope-sizing note: this task is at the upper bound of complexity 6. If during implementation it feels larger (e.g., the fixture becomes a combinatorial explosion or parse error cases multiply), the implementing agent MAY decompose into two subtasks: (a) render + hash + barrel export; (b) parse + fixture + roundtrip test. Decide at RED-phase when the test count becomes unwieldy — do not rewrite work.

tasks.title: Editor subprocess helper — spawn, TTY/CI guard, tempfile management
tasks.description: Add pure I/O plumbing for launching the user's editor against a temp file. Isolated from task-model knowledge — the subprocess helper takes a string and returns a string, nothing else. Keeps the Phase 2 main loop (FORGE-4.3/4.4) thin.
tasks.design:
Add to src/lib/tasks/editor.ts (same module as render/parse from FORGE-4.1):

  export interface RunEditorOptions {
    editorOverride?: string;    // --editor <cmd>; else $VISUAL → $EDITOR → 'vi'
    tmpdir?: string;            // for tests; default os.tmpdir()
  }

  export function runEditor(
    initialContent: string,
    opts?: RunEditorOptions,
  ): string;  // returns the post-edit buffer content (possibly empty)

  export function assertInteractive(): void;
  // Throws a typed error if !process.stdout.isTTY or process.env.CI === 'true'.
  // Error message: 'forge tasks edit requires an interactive terminal; CI/non-TTY environments are not supported'.

Behavior for runEditor:
  - Create a temp file under opts.tmpdir ?? os.tmpdir(). Use a prefix like 'forge-edit-' and suffix '.md' so editors pick up markdown syntax highlighting. Use mkdtempSync-style uniqueness.
  - Write initialContent to the temp file (utf-8).
  - Resolve the editor command: opts.editorOverride ?? process.env.VISUAL ?? process.env.EDITOR ?? 'vi'.
  - Spawn synchronously with { stdio: 'inherit' } so the user's terminal drives the editor directly. Use child_process.spawnSync.
  - After the editor exits: if exit code is non-zero, throw with a message naming the editor and the exit code. tasks.json is not this module's concern.
  - Read the final file contents, delete the temp file (ignore errors on cleanup), return the contents.

assertInteractive is a separate export so callers can gate early (before rendering the buffer). Called at the top of the main handler in FORGE-4.3.

Testing strategy: the subprocess is genuinely hard to unit-test without a real editor. Strategies:
  - Test runEditor with editorOverride set to a shell command that modifies the file deterministically, e.g. 'sh -c "echo MODIFIED > \"\\""' (note: spawnSync doesn't invoke a shell, so prefer a node one-liner: 'node -e "require(\"fs\").writeFileSync(process.argv[1], \"MODIFIED\")"'). Verify the return value equals the post-modification contents.
  - Test the editor-not-found / non-zero-exit path via an override like 'false' or 'node -e "process.exit(1)"'.
  - Test assertInteractive by monkeypatching process.stdout.isTTY and process.env.CI.
  - Tempfile cleanup: assert the temp file path does not exist after runEditor returns successfully.

No CLI handler, no task-model coupling, no hash/lock logic here. That's all downstream.
acceptance: runEditor with a deterministic editor override that appends 'X' to the initial content returns the initial content + 'X'
runEditor with an editor override that exits non-zero throws with a message naming the editor command and the exit code; the temp file is cleaned up
runEditor deletes the temp file after a successful read (test by capturing the path via a spy and asserting !fs.existsSync afterwards)
Editor selection precedence: editorOverride beats $VISUAL; $VISUAL beats $EDITOR; $EDITOR beats the 'vi' fallback. Cover each hop with a test using process.env manipulation
assertInteractive throws when process.stdout.isTTY is false
assertInteractive throws when process.env.CI === 'true'
assertInteractive returns void (doesn't throw) when isTTY is truthy and CI is unset
Error message from assertInteractive contains the substring 'forge tasks edit requires an interactive terminal'
Tests live in src/lib/__tests__/tasks-editor.test.ts (extending FORGE-4.1's file), not in a nested __tests__ dir
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes

Multi-arg editor support — ='code --wait', ='subl -w', and similar multi-word editor commands are common. runEditor MUST invoke the resolved editor command through a shell so multi-token strings split correctly. Recommended: spawnSync('sh', ['-c', `${editor} "$1"`, 'sh', tmpFilePath], { stdio: 'inherit' }) — this is git's approach (GIT_EDITOR) and correctly handles both single-token (vim) and multi-token (code --wait) editors, including ones with spaces in paths. The 'no shell interpolation' rule in the original design applies to USER content in task buffers (mutations); editor invocation is our own trusted tooling. Add tests: one for single-token editor override, one for a multi-token editor override that modifies the buffer.
tasks.notes:
Files: src/lib/tasks/editor.ts (modify — FORGE-4.1 creates it, this task appends subprocess helpers), src/lib/__tests__/tasks-editor.test.ts (extend).

Because both FORGE-4.1 and this task write to src/lib/tasks/editor.ts, this task is now sequenced AFTER FORGE-4.1 (dep added) to avoid merge conflicts in the same file.

Node APIs: child_process.spawnSync, fs, os.tmpdir, path. Do NOT use child_process.exec / shell interpolation — spawnSync with an argv array only.

Deterministic editor mock for tests: use a node one-liner via spawnSync. Example that appends 'X' to the buffer:

  const mock = [process.execPath, '-e', 'const f=require("fs");const p=process.argv[1];f.writeFileSync(p,f.readFileSync(p,"utf8")+"X")'];
  runEditor(initial, { editorOverride: mock.join(' ') });

(If spawnSync can take an argv array, prefer passing an array-typed override rather than a pre-joined string. Choose whichever shape makes the runEditor signature cleaner; document the choice.)

The process.env mutations in tests MUST use beforeEach/afterEach to restore state between tests (VISUAL / EDITOR / CI).

tasks.title: forge tasks edit — core flow, dispatcher, and --dry-run
tasks.description: Wire up the forge tasks edit CLI subcommand for the HAPPY path: render task → spawn editor → parse buffer → apply edits to tasks.json. Covers unchanged-buffer abort, empty-buffer abort, --dry-run (no write), and the TTY/CI guard. Robustness features — crontab-style re-open on errors, optimistic lock, --force — land in FORGE-4.4 and extend this task's handler rather than rewrite it.
tasks.design:
Add to src/commands/tasks.ts:

  - RESERVED: append 'edit'
  - VALUE_FLAGS: append '--editor'
  - BOOLEAN_FLAGS: append '--dry-run'  ('--force' already present)
  - Register 'edit' in the subcommand dispatcher
  - handleEdit(args) orchestration function

### Prerequisite fix (lands in THIS task's commit)

Before wiring handleEdit, patch src/lib/tasks/mutations.ts to allow replaceAcceptance to accept an empty array. Current code at line 420 reads `if (fields.addAcceptance && fields.addAcceptance.length > 0)` — which skips the overwrite when the user clears acceptance in the buffer.

Required change: when replaceAcceptance === true, honor it even for addAcceptance = [] (task.acceptance becomes []). Preserve the existing append-on-empty-flag no-op semantics (if replaceAcceptance is NOT set and addAcceptance is empty, do nothing — keep existing tests green).

Pseudocode:
  if (fields.replaceAcceptance && fields.addAcceptance !== undefined) {
    task.acceptance = [...fields.addAcceptance];    // may be []
  } else if (fields.addAcceptance && fields.addAcceptance.length > 0) {
    for (const ac of fields.addAcceptance) task.acceptance.push(ac);
  }

Add a lib-level regression test: updateTask with replaceAcceptance:true and addAcceptance:[] on a task with prior criteria → resulting task.acceptance === []. Keep every existing mutations test green (FORGE-3.7's append path must not regress).

### handleEdit structure (hooks FORGE-4.4 will extend without rewrite)

1. Call assertInteractive() (from FORGE-4.2). No-TTY / CI → catch and exit 1.
2. Parse args: extract --editor <cmd>, --dry-run, --force (parse now so 4.4 just reads the value), positional task-id.
3. Look up task across feature files (same pattern handleShow / handleUpdate use). Unknown id → exit 1 with clear message. If the id resolves to an epic → exit 1 with 'cannot edit epics; edit their child tasks, or use forge tasks update <epic-id> for individual fields'.
4. HOOK for 4.4: `const openHash = hashTask(task); void openHash;` — compute but only reference in 4.4. The `void openHash` line prevents unused-binding lint errors. 4.4 will replace it with the actual comparison.
5. renderBuffer(task) → initialBuffer.
6. runEditor(initialBuffer, { editorOverride }) → editedContent.
7. Empty buffer (post-trim): print 'edit aborted (empty buffer)' to stderr; exit 0; no write.
8. Unchanged buffer (strict equality with initialBuffer, or value-equality of the two after stripping non-editable header): print 'edit aborted (no changes)' to stderr; exit 0; no write.
9. parseBuffer(editedContent). On throw: handler surfaces the error and exits 1. (4.4 adds the crontab retry here.)
10. Emit parseResult.warnings to stderr as 'warning: <text>'.
11. HOOK for 4.4: lock check (skip in this task).
12. Write updates via updateTask with fields: { title, priority, labels, dependencies, description, design, notes, addAcceptance: parseResult.task.acceptance, replaceAcceptance: true }. Relies on the mutations.ts patch above.
13. If --dry-run: skip the write; print a field-by-field diff to stdout ('FIELD: <before> -> <after>' per changed field; skip unchanged). Exit 0.
14. Else: write; print 'Updated FORGE-X.Y'; exit 0.

### Help text (edit)
  forge tasks edit <task-id> [--editor <cmd>] [--dry-run] [--force]
  --editor <cmd>  Override $VISUAL/$EDITOR (default: $VISUAL -> $EDITOR -> vi)
  --dry-run       Render + edit + parse + diff, but do not write
  --force         Overwrite on concurrency conflict (see FORGE-4.4)

### Integration tests in src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts

Mock the editor via --editor pointing at a node one-liner that applies a deterministic transform to the buffer. Cover:
  - Happy path: modify title → task updated
  - Unchanged buffer: no write; tasks.json byte-identical
  - Empty buffer: exit 0 with 'empty buffer' message; no write; no delete invoked
  - Clear acceptance: buffer rendered with acceptance items, editor removes them all; after save, task.acceptance === [] (this tests the mutations.ts patch above)
  - Unknown task id: exit 1 with clear message
  - Editing an epic id: exit 1 with the prescribed error message (see step 3 above)
  - Unknown frontmatter key in edited buffer: exit 1 with a parse error naming the key
  - --dry-run: buffer modified → tasks.json byte-identical; diff printed to stdout
  - No-TTY guard: stub isTTY=false → fails BEFORE any editor spawn
  - CI=true guard: same triggered via CI env var

### Anti-goals for 4.3
  - No crontab re-open loop (4.4)
  - No optimistic-lock conflict detection / --force active behavior (4.4)
  - No dependency-existence validation (4.4 — parse still catches shape errors; 4.4 adds the ID-scan)

### PR-packaging note
FORGE-4.3 and FORGE-4.4 are co-designed so that 4.3 leaves hooks for 4.4. Shipping 4.3 WITHOUT 4.4 is strictly worse than status-quo (no concurrency protection, full-field overwrite after minutes of user typing). The two tasks MUST land in the same PR. This is naturally satisfied by the phase's phase-prs execution mode (one PR per phase), but call it out in the PR body.

Depends on FORGE-4.1 (renderBuffer, parseBuffer) and FORGE-4.2 (runEditor, assertInteractive). tasks.title: forge tasks edit robustness — crontab re-open, optimistic lock, --force
tasks.description: Extend FORGE-4.3's handleEdit with robustness features: crontab-style re-open loop on parse/validation errors (preserves user edits across retries), dependency-existence validation, optimistic locking via hashTask, and --force behavior on conflict. This is the user-cant-lose-work task.
tasks.design:
Extend handleEdit in src/commands/tasks.ts (do NOT rewrite — hook into the spots FORGE-4.3 left).

### PR-packaging note
FORGE-4.3 and this task MUST ship in the same PR. 4.3 alone is strictly worse than status-quo (editor opens outside a lock, full-field overwrite after minutes of typing). Phase-prs mode handles this naturally; document in the phase PR body.

### 1. Optimistic lock on open

- Replace 4.3's `const openHash = hashTask(task); void openHash;` with an active binding.
- At the hook after parse (4.3 step 11): re-read tasks.json, find the same task by id. Compute newHash over the reloaded task.
- If openHash !== newHash AND --force is NOT set:
    * Print a field-level diff to stderr showing what changed server-side (reuse the diff-printer from --dry-run).
    * Print: "concurrent write detected. re-run with --force to overwrite, or exit-without-saving (already happened — no write occurred) to abort".
    * Exit 1. No write.
- If --force is set, skip the check; proceed to write, overwriting the concurrent change.

### 2. Crontab-style re-open loop

- Wrap the parse step (4.3 step 9) in a retry loop (max 5 iterations).
- On parse error: re-invoke runEditor with a modified buffer that prepends a SENTINEL error block. The block has a fixed shape:

    # forge:error-block-start
    # edit failed — fix the issues below and save again, or quit to abort:
    #
    #   - <error message>
    #
    # forge:error-block-end

- Use the sentinel markers (`# forge:error-block-start` / `# forge:error-block-end`) so stripping on retry is unambiguous: strip the first contiguous range from `# forge:error-block-start` to `# forge:error-block-end` (inclusive, plus trailing blank line). Do NOT strip other lines starting with `#` — the renderBuffer output has its own leading reference header (also `#` comments) that must be preserved verbatim.
- Each retry calls runEditor afresh (runEditor creates and deletes its own tempfile per call — see FORGE-4.2). The buffer content is the ONLY state that persists across retries.
- Empty buffer on retry: treat as abort (exit 0, no write — same as 4.3 step 7).
- Retries exhausted (5 parse failures in a row): exit 1 with message matching /too many parse failures/; tasks.json unmodified.

### 3. Dependency-existence validation

- After parseBuffer succeeds (every iteration of the retry loop), before the lock check: for every id in parseResult.task.dependencies, verify the id exists across all task files (reuse the cross-feature id-scan pattern from FORGE-3.3's mutations.ts).
- If any dependencies are unknown: throw a validation error that names every unknown id (batch, matching FORGE-3.3 style). The retry loop catches this throw and feeds it back as the next error block.

### 4. --dry-run interaction

- --dry-run SKIPS the lock check (dry runs are read-only).
- --dry-run is SINGLE-SHOT — a parse error in --dry-run just prints and exits 1 WITHOUT entering the retry loop.
- This preserves 4.3's --dry-run behavior unchanged.

### Integration tests (src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts)

- Concurrency conflict: edit-in-progress, simulate a concurrent tasks.json write (direct JSON edit on disk before the save) → exit 1 with conflict diff on stderr; tasks.json reflects the concurrent write.
- Concurrency conflict + --force: same setup, --force passed → write succeeds; tasks.json reflects the edit's changes.
- Parse-error re-open: editor mock returns invalid-then-valid buffers; runEditor invoked twice; the second invocation's initialContent contains the sentinel error block; final tasks.json reflects the valid buffer.
- Dependency validation: buffer with unknown dep id → reopen with unknown-id error; retry with valid deps → write succeeds.
- Batch dependency errors: buffer with multiple unknown deps → error message names all of them.
- Max retries: editor mock always produces an invalid buffer; after 5 attempts, exit 1; tasks.json unmodified.
- Sentinel stripping (critical): a 3-retry scenario's final buffer contains exactly zero `# forge:error-block-start` lines; the non-editable reference header lines (rendered by 4.1's renderBuffer) ARE preserved across retries.
- --dry-run + parse error: prints error; exits 1; does NOT loop.
- --dry-run + stale tasks.json: skips lock check; still performs parse + diff.
acceptance: Optimistic lock: run two edits sequentially where the second edits a task that was modified by a direct tasks.json write after the first edit started; second edit exits 1 with a conflict diff on stderr; tasks.json reflects the concurrent write (unchanged by the aborted edit)
Optimistic lock + --force: same conflict scenario with --force; second edit overwrites; tasks.json reflects the edit's changes

Parse-error re-open: editor mock returns invalid-then-valid buffers; runEditor is invoked twice; the second invocation's initialContent contains a leading comment block naming the first error; final tasks.json reflects the valid buffer's content
Dependency validation: editor produces a buffer with an unknown dep id; re-open loop shows the unknown-id error; on retry with valid deps, write succeeds
Batch dependency errors: buffer with multiple unknown dep ids produces an error message naming all of them (match FORGE-3.3 batch-error style)
Max retries: editor mock always produces an invalid buffer; after 5 attempts, command exits 1 with a message containing 'too many parse failures'; tasks.json is unmodified
Comment-block stripping: verify no nested error blocks accumulate across retries (a 3-retry scenario's final buffer contains zero leading '# edit failed' lines)
--dry-run + parse error: prints the error and exits 1 WITHOUT entering the retry loop (dry-run is single-shot)
--dry-run + stale hash: even if tasks.json changes mid-flight, --dry-run does not perform the lock check
pnpm typecheck passes; pnpm test passes

Sentinel stripping precision: the non-editable reference header rendered by renderBuffer (also '#' comments) is PRESERVED across all retries; only the '# forge:error-block-start' ... '# forge:error-block-end' ranges are stripped
Merge-gate: FORGE-4.4 is NOT individually mergeable. It may only land on main in a PR that ALSO includes FORGE-4.3 closed. The two tasks are a single atomic release.

Retry-abort UX — when the user saves an UNCHANGED buffer during a retry iteration (after seeing an error), emit a distinct message like 'edit aborted (no changes after error — did you mean to fix the issue?)' rather than the first-shot 'edit aborted (no changes)'. Still exit 0 with no write; the differentiation is just to avoid UX confusion about why the abort happened.
tasks.notes: Files: src/commands/tasks.ts (modify — extend handleEdit at the hooks FORGE-4.3 left), src/commands/__tests__/tasks.test.ts (extend). Depends on FORGE-4.1 (hashTask), FORGE-4.2 (runEditor), FORGE-4.3 (handleEdit structure and hooks). The hashTask call happens over the in-memory Task after reloading tasks.json — not over the buffer content. Reading tasks.json is not itself atomic wrt concurrent writes by another process, but the optimistic-lock pattern accepts that tradeoff.
